ElementDescription: The NDAR Global Unique Identifier (GUID) for research subject
Subject ID how it's defined in lab/project
Date on which the interview/genetic test/sampling/imaging/biospecimen was completed. MM/DD/YYYY
Age in months at the time of the interview/test/sampling/imaging.
Notes: Age is rounded to chronological month. If the research participant is 15-days-old at time of interview, the appropriate value would be 0 months. If the participant is 16-days-old, the value would be 1 month.

ElementDescription: Sex of subject at birth
Notes: M = Male; F = Female; O=Other; NR = Not reported

ElementDescription: Form used/assessment name
Notes: e.g., Self-Report, Parent Form, Short Form, Revised Version, Other Informant Form

ElementDescription: This child... Fusses or shuts eyes in bright light.
This child... Has difficulty spotting a favorite toy when it is lying among other objects.
This child... Stares at spinning objects or lights that flicker or flash.
This child... Has trouble following moving objects with his or her eyes.
This child... Looks at objects out of the corner of his or her eye.
This child... Fails to notice bright or colorful toys.
This child... Shows distress in brightly colored rooms.
This child... Has difficulty finding a toy that is lying on a patterned rug or blanket.
This child... Stares intently at people or objects.
This child... Stares at bright lights.
This child... Shows distress at ordinary household sounds, such as a vacuum cleaner or a toilet flushing.
This child... Notices subtle sounds that others don't, such as a distant airplane or a humming refrigerator.
This child... Fails to look at me when I call his or her name.
This child... Shows distress at unexpected or loud noises.
This child... Has difficulty sleeping with quiet background noise, such as footsteps or clicking of a computer keyboard.
This child... Seems confused about where sounds are coming from.
This child... Listens intently to the sounds he or she makes, while ignoring everything else.
This child... Seems to enjoy loud noises that irritate others.
This child... Likes making certain sounds over and over again, such as repeatedly flushing the toilet.
This child... Shows distress when hearing busy sounds from a party or a crowded room.
This child... Shows distress when touching foods with fingers.
This child... Fusses unless the temperature of food, bathwater, or a wet washcloth is just right.
This child... Fails to notice sensations that would be painful to others.
This child... Fusses while being dressed or diapered.
This child... Fails to notice that food is in his or her mouth.
This child... Seeks out sensations that would be painful to others, such as crashing onto the floor.
This child... Seeks out being wrapped in tight blankets or clothing.
This child... Shows distress, gags, or vomits at certain food textures, such as mushy or crunchy foods.
This child... Seeks out small spaces to burrow under, such as a pile of pillows.
This child... Puts hands, head, or body against vibrating objects, such as a dishwasher or washing machine.
This child... Smells toys or other nonfood objects.
This child... Shows distress at the scent of soap, perfume, or skin lotions.
This child... Fails to notice strong or unusual smells.
This child... Gags at certain smells.
This child... Prefers foods with strong smells.
This child... Avoids foods with strong smells.
This child... Fails to show preference for one food taste over another.
This child... Avoids foods with strong flavors.
This child... Gags when tasting foods.
This child... Insists on eating the same brand of a particular food.
This child... Plays roughly with objects, pets, or other children.
This child... Deliberately bangs head into objects or people.
This child... Chews on toys, clothes, or other objects.
This child... Breaks toys by handling them with too much force.
This child... Moves slowly or sluggishly.
This child... Walks on toes.
This child... Goes limp when picked up by an adult.
This child... Drops a lot of food while eating with a spoon.
This child... Bites or hits self or others.
This child... Tires easily during feeding.
This child... Gets carsick.
This child... Slouches or slumps forward while sitting.
This child... Needs intense movement to calm down.
This child... Fails to catch himself or herself when falling.
This child... Needs movement to fall asleep, such as rocking or riding in a car.
This child... Seeks out intense or continuous movement, such as swinging, rocking, or bouncing.
This child... Leans on people or furniture to stay upright.
This child... Fusses when head goes upside down.
This child... Can spin around many times without getting dizzy.
This child... Fails to jump off the ground using both feet.
This child... Fails to explore new ways to play with familiar objects.
This child... Uses the same pattern repeatedly when playing with toys or objects, such as lining up items in the same way.
This child... Fails to put one object inside another when playing, such as putting a block in a cup.
This child... Has difficulty climbing down from a chair, couch, or high surface.
This child... Has difficulty climbing onto a chair or car seat and turning around to sit.
This child... Has difficulty managing foods or liquids in mouth.
This child... Has difficulty maneuvering around, under, or over obstacles.
This child... Repeats the same activity over and over with no improvement in skill.
This child... Has trouble accurately imitating sounds, such as blowing raspberries or saying ''mama.''
This child... Has trouble accurately imitating facial expressions or mouth or tongue movements.
This child... Responds to my voice or face with vocal or facial expressions.
This child... Tries to give or take objects to and from adults.
This child... Points at objects to show them to adults.
This child... Is easily calmed by a familiar adult when upset.
This child... Expresses a range of emotions, such as joy, sadness, and frustration.
This child... Looks into my eyes for more than a quick glance when I speak to him or her.
This child... Easily goes along with an unexpected change in routine.
This child... Sleeps without disrupting the family routine.
This child... Leans forward and opens mouth during feeding.
This child... Checks to make sure that I am nearby when in a new place.
